content.text: Control flow is the mechanism that drives an agent forward from a user request to a final answer. Unlike a conventional program that follows a fixed execution path, an agent loop is conditional at every turn: the model decides whether to call a tool, return a result, or ask for clarification, and the loop infrastructure routes each decision to the appropriate next step. Understanding the exact sequence of that loop — and where it can branch or terminate — is prerequisite knowledge for building reliable agents.

attrs.alt: Agent control flow — the agentic loop from task to response

content.text: The following is a minimal but complete agentic loop. Every production agent is a variation of this pattern:
Three things are worth noting about this loop. First,
grows by exactly two items per iteration: one assistant turn and one user turn containing tool results. Second,
is the only authoritative exit signal — not the presence or absence of tool calls. Third, the
guard prevents infinite loops when the model gets stuck in a cycle.
The API returns one of four
strings. Your loop must handle each one explicitly:
Model decided it is done — returned a text response or called a final tool.
Return the text to the caller. Loop exits.
One or more tool_use blocks are present. Model is waiting for results.
Execute each tool, collect results, append as user turn, loop again.
Response was cut mid-generation. Content is incomplete.
Trim context or summarize history. Do not continue as-is.
A custom stop sequence was hit.
Parse the partial response per your protocol.
, there may be more than one
tool_use block receives a corresponding tool_result in the next user turn — even if some tools failed. An incomplete set of results will cause an API validation error.

Parallel dispatch is safe when the tools do not depend on each other's results. If tool B depends on the output of tool A, the model will call them in separate turns — it will not request them in the same batch.
The messages list must alternate strictly between
roles. Tool results are appended as
turns — not as a separate message type. A turn that contains tool results may also contain text, but in practice you rarely mix them.

# Correct message structure after two loop iterations:
# messages = [
#   {"role": "user",      "content": "Summarise the open issues."},   # initial task
#   {"role": "assistant", "content": [
#       {"type": "text",     "text": "Let me check the repository."},
#       {"type": "tool_use", "id": "tu_1", "name": "list_issues", "input": {}},
#   ]},
#   {"role": "user",      "content": [
#       {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "tu_1",
#        "content": '[{"number": 42, "title": "Login fails"}]'},
#   ]},
#   {"role": "assistant", "content": [
#       {"type": "text", "text": "There is one open issue: #42 — Login fails."},
#   ]},  # stop_reason: end_turn
# ]

# Common mistake: forgetting that response.content is already a list
def append_assistant_turn(messages: list, response) -> None:
    # CORRECT — response.content is list[ContentBlock]
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})

def append_tool_results(messages: list, tool_results: list[dict]) -> None:
    # tool_results is a list of tool_result dicts
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

A well-behaved model will stop in a few turns for simple tasks and a few dozen turns for complex ones. But loops can run away in two failure modes: the model calls the same failing tool repeatedly, or a bug in your dispatch layer returns outputs that confuse the model into repeating itself.
A hard turn cap is the last line of defence, but a more informative guard also tracks consecutive identical tool calls:
Before the first API call, you assemble the initial

list. This is the correct place to inject retrieved memory, prior run summaries, and any structured context the model needs before it begins reasoning. Injecting context inside the loop (as a fake user turn after turn 0) works but is harder to reason about.

For long-running agents, streaming lets you surface partial text to the user in real time without waiting for the full response. The loop structure is identical — you accumulate streamed blocks into the same
list before deciding the next step:
. Treating a truncated response like a normal
Omitting a tool_result for a tool_use block. The API will reject the next request with a 400 error — every
, a model stuck in a retry cycle runs indefinitely, accumulating cost.
Swallowing tool errors silently. Returning an empty string instead of an error message leaves the model with no signal that anything went wrong, so it will likely repeat the same call.
before appending it. The SDK's content list is what the API expects back unchanged — always append the raw object.
You can test control flow logic without making real API calls by replacing the client with a fake that returns scripted responses:
or a hard turn limit is hit.
Every tool_use block must be answered with a tool_result in the next user turn — the API enforces this.
Multiple tool calls in a single turn can be dispatched in parallel when they are independent.
explicitly — a truncated response is not a completed one.
Always return error details from tool dispatch — silent failures cause the model to repeat itself.
